channel.title: Orthodox Christian Sermons: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost
channel.description: Audio Orthodox Christian Sermons: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost

item.title: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost, The light of the body is the eye., 2008-07-06
item.description: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. The light of the body is the eye., 2008-07-06
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. The light of the body is the eye., 2008-07-06

item.title: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost, Patience and the eye of the soul., 2009-06-28
item.description: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. Patience and the eye of the soul., 2009-06-28
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. Patience and the eye of the soul., 2009-06-28

item.title: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost, Answer All The Questions, 2010-06-13
item.description: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. The Scripture is full of questions, commands, warnings, promises, and consequences for actions. All of these, every single one of them, applies to us. You must read the Scripture and answer the questions and listen to the commands and heed the warnings and realize they are for you. Here is one: the Lord says: Is not life more than meat and the body more than raiment?” That question is a very important one to answer, and it’s one that actually is answered hundreds of times every day by every person, whether they are Christian or not, whether or not they believe in God. And with it is the question that follows: “And why do you take thought for your clothing?” None of this makes any sense to anyone unless we answer this first question well. This question can be also stated: What is life all about? ...
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. The Scripture is full of questions, commands, warnings, promises, and consequences for actions. All of these, every single one of them, applies to us. You must read the Scripture and answer the questions and listen to the commands and heed the warnings and realize they are for you. Here is one: the Lord says: Is not life more than meat and the body more than raiment?” That question is a very important one to answer, and it’s one that actually is answered hundreds of times every day by every person, whether they are Christian or not, whether or not they believe in God. And with it is the question that follows: “And why do you take thought for your clothing?” None of this makes any sense to anyone unless we answer this first question well. This question can be also stated: What is life all about? ...

item.title: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost, How To Discern Light And Darkness In The Eye Of The Soul, 2011-07-03
item.description: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. Today we learn about desire, and what shapes our desires. Just as the light of the body is the eye, the soul also has an eye that enlightens it. Our physical eyes can easily discern light and darkness, but the eye of the soul must be trained to do this, because without training. The eye of the soul will not know light from darkness. How do we train the eye of the soul?
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. Today we learn about desire, and what shapes our desires. Just as the light of the body is the eye, the soul also has an eye that enlightens it. Our physical eyes can easily discern light and darkness, but the eye of the soul must be trained to do this, because without training. The eye of the soul will not know light from darkness. How do we train the eye of the soul?

item.title: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost, 2012-06-24
item.description: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. The Lord never gives us a command that He does not explain and teach how to fulfill. In this selection, He first teaches us how to fulfill the command by teaching about the eye, and desire, and then progresses to rebuke our thoughts and anxieties. These things are obviously related! They both tie in to HOW to fulfill the command "Seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness".
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. The Lord never gives us a command that He does not explain and teach how to fulfill. In this selection, He first teaches us how to fulfill the command by teaching about the eye, and desire, and then progresses to rebuke our thoughts and anxieties. These things are obviously related! They both tie in to HOW to fulfill the command "Seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness".

item.title: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost, 2013-07-14
item.description: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. There is always a subtext to Jesus' commands to us. In the case of His command to "Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink; nor yet for your body, what ye shall put on", this command is preceded by a discussion about desire, in which He tells us "The light of the body is the eye: if therefore thine eye be single, thy whole body shall be full of light." and He warns us that we cannot serve God and mammon. This juxtaposition of teaching about desire and the commandment to not be anxious should cause a "Eureka!" moment for us - unbridled desire ALWAYS leads to anxiety. We look at this in detail .
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. There is always a subtext to Jesus' commands to us. In the case of His command to "Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink; nor yet for your body, what ye shall put on", this command is preceded by a discussion about desire, in which He tells us "The light of the body is the eye: if therefore thine eye be single, thy whole body shall be full of light." and He warns us that we cannot serve God and mammon. This juxtaposition of teaching about desire and the commandment to not be anxious should cause a "Eureka!" moment for us - unbridled desire ALWAYS leads to anxiety. We look at this in detail .

item.title: 3rd Sunday after Pentecost, 2014-06-29
item.description: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. We must answer all the questions in Scripture; all are intended to teach us, challenge us, enlighten us. The questions the Lord asks in his teaching which begins "The light of the body is the eye" are designed to teach us the meaning of life and how to attain it. He begins by teaching about desire, which is a God-given attribute of the soul, since God has desire, and has made us in His image, and that this desire can be used for good or bad. Desire is good, when it is used for good, that is when we understand what life is and how to live our life according to the only proper answer to the question: "Is not the life more than meat, and the body than raiment?" We control and perfect our life through our thoughts. All good and bad things in our life begin with thought. the Lord teaches us how to think, if we are able to answer the question: " Which of you by taking thought can add one cubit unto his stature? 28 And why take ye thought for raiment?" Let us answer the questions the Lord asks us about the meaning and purpose of our life, and also learn how to attain to this purpose.
item.itunes:author: Priest Seraphim Holland
item.itunes:summary: :3rd Sunday after Pentecost. We must answer all the questions in Scripture; all are intended to teach us, challenge us, enlighten us. The questions the Lord asks in his teaching which begins "The light of the body is the eye" are designed to teach us the meaning of life and how to attain it. He begins by teaching about desire, which is a God-given attribute of the soul, since God has desire, and has made us in His image, and that this desire can be used for good or bad. Desire is good, when it is used for good, that is when we understand what life is and how to live our life according to the only proper answer to the question: "Is not the life more than meat, and the body than raiment?" We control and perfect our life through our thoughts. All good and bad things in our life begin with thought. the Lord teaches us how to think, if we are able to answer the question: " Which of you by taking thought can add one cubit unto his stature? 28 And why take ye thought for raiment?" Let us answer the questions the Lord asks us about the meaning and purpose of our life, and also learn how to attain to this purpose.
